fact: July 5 is exactly 186 days into the year - that's more than half the year gone already!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when fireflies are typically at their flashiest, blinking like tiny disco balls in your backyard every night.
Down in Antarctica, July 5 falls in the middle of winter when it's so dark that the sun doesn't rise at all - it's like living inside a freezer with the lights off!
Cherry trees in northern climates like Canada are often dropping their fruit right now, creating squishy red carpets on the ground.
The sunrise happens around 5:30 AM in many northern cities, painting the sky bright orange and pink.
Cicadas are usually screaming their loudest on hot July days like this one in North America - some species buzz so loud they can be heard from far away!
In Australia and other southern places, July 5 is deep winter when animals like wombats grow their fluffiest fur coats to stay warm.
This date falls right in Cancer season for zodiac fans, which means the moon's influence is supposed to be extra strong.
Mosquitoes are typically at their most active around July 5 in swampy areas of the Northern Hemisphere because the hot, humid weather helps them breed.
Ocean water in the Northern Hemisphere often reaches some of its warmest temperatures right around now, making it perfect for swimming.
July 5 sits almost exactly one month after the summer solstice, which means daylight is still super long but starting its slow shrink toward winter.
Desert lizards in places like Arizona typically do a hot-foot dance on July days, lifting their feet up and down to avoid getting burned on the scorching sand!
